description: The image displays the CrossMark publication identifier, consisting of a red bookmark-shaped icon enclosed within a blue circular border, accompanied by the text label 'CrossMark' to the right. This icon represents a cross-publisher initiative (primarily by Elsevier and other major scientific publishers) designed to indicate the version-of-record status of scholarly articles and provide transparency regarding updates, corrections, retractions, or editorial changes post-publication. Unlike conventional material characterization figures, this is a metadata marker rather than a scientific data visualization; it does not display microstructural features, diffraction patterns, mechanical property curves, or synthesis routes of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The CrossMark logo typically appears in the header, footer, or margin of published academic papers and functions as a clickable link to the publisher's article status database, allowing readers to verify the current state of the document. This figure serves administrative and publishing integrity purposes rather than conveying experimental results or material science data.

Figure 1 comprises two sub-panels quantifying the effects of alloying additions on the physical properties of ferritic iron. Panel (a) plots the density reduction as a function of alloy content (0–16 wt%), showing that carbon and aluminum produce the steepest decreases (density drops from ~7.87 g/cm³ to ~7.0 g/cm³ for 2 wt% C and to ~6.1 g/cm³ for 16 wt% Al), whereas chromium and manganese exhibit gentle negative slopes. Panel (b) illustrates the degradation of Young’s modulus in annealed Fe-Al steels with increasing aluminum content (0–16 wt%), compiling experimental data from Frommeyer [24], Kubo [28], Yamamoto-Taniguchi [28], and Fukuroi-Shibuya [28]. The modulus declines from ~215 GPa to approximately 120–160 GPa, with a quantified reduction rate of 2–2.5% per 1 wt% Al attributed to decreased lattice energy and increased atomic spacing. Together, these charts establish the fundamental compromise in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels: aluminum addition reduces density favorably for lightweight automotive applications but simultaneously lowers the elastic modulus unfavorably, limiting specific stiffness performance.

The figure plots Young's modulus (E) versus manganese content (5–40 wt%) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, comparing three processing conditions measured via the resonance method. The as-cast state (blue curve, Fe-(5–40)Mn-10Al-1C) exhibits the lowest modulus values, ranging from approximately 143 to 152 GPa, which caption states is due to casting defects such as shrinkage cavities and interdendritic segregations. The as-hot rolled condition (red curve, Fe-(15–28)Mn-(7–10)Al-(0.7–1.2)C) displays the highest modulus (~168–180 GPa), attributed to the healing of micro-cavities during reheating and deformation. The as-cold rolled and annealed state (solid black line with shaded uncertainty band representing Fe-(15–28)Mn-(7–10)Al-(0.7–1.2)C) shows intermediate values (~158–166 GPa), slightly reduced from the hot-rolled condition, reflecting the influence of cold work and texture changes upon annealing. All curves demonstrate that increasing Mn content generally stabilizes or slightly decreases the modulus within each processing state. Collectively, the data reveal that thermomechanical processing significantly enhances Young's modulus relative to the as-cast state, yet all Fe-Mn-Al-C compositions maintain values (~140–180 GPa) substantially below conventional HSLA grades (~210 GPa), supporting their development as lightweight structural materials.

This figure presents a comparative bar chart illustrating the normalized specific stiffness (specific stiffness relative to aluminum) of four engineering materials: Aluminium (6xxx series), HSLA steel, Fe-8Al ferritic steel, and Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C austenitic steel. The x-axis displays three distinct structural indices corresponding to different component geometries: n=1 for ties (E/ρ), n=1/2 for beams (√E/ρ), and n=1/3 for panels (∛E/ρ). The y-axis shows the specific stiffness normalized against aluminum. The chart reveals that aluminum maintains the highest specific stiffness across all three loading conditions, with values at or above 1.0. HSLA steel shows comparable specific stiffness to aluminum only for the tie configuration (n=1), but drops significantly for beams and panels. The low-density steels (Fe-8Al and Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C) exhibit progressively lower specific stiffness values, with the Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C alloy showing the lowest values among the four materials. As the geometric index n decreases from 1 to 1/3, the relative specific stiffness of all steels decreases substantially, while aluminum remains constant at 1.0. This visualization supports the critical conclusion that low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels do not offer superior specific stiffness compared to conventional HSLA steels or aluminum alloys, indicating their application should be limited to structural components where stiffness is not the primary design constraint.

This figure displays the iron-rich portion of the binary Fe-Al phase diagram adapted from Kubaschewski [88], providing the essential thermodynamic framework for understanding phase stability in ferritic Fe-Al steels and Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The vertical axis represents temperature ranging from 200°C to 1600°C, while the horizontal axis shows aluminum concentration from 0 to 40 wt%. The diagram delineates distinct phase fields including liquid (L), high-temperature austenite (γ, FCC), disordered body-centered cubic ferrite (α, A2), and ordered intermetallic compounds FeAl (B2) and Fe₃Al (DO₃). Critical invariant temperatures are explicitly annotated: 1536°C (pure iron melting point), 1392°C and 911°C (austenite-to-ferrite transformations), 1310°C (peritectic reaction), 1215°C and 1092°C (order-disorder transitions), and 552°C (eutectoid decomposition). Additionally, the magnetic transformation boundary and the 'K-state' region (K₁, K₂)—associated with short-range atomic ordering and anomalous resistivity behavior—are indicated. This phase diagram is crucial for predicting disorder-order transitions (BCC_A2 to BCC_B2/DO₃) that govern mechanical properties and embrittlement in aluminum-alloyed steels.

This figure presents three vertical-section phase diagrams illustrating the effects of Mn and Al additions on the phase constituent and phase fields of the Fe-C system. Panel (a) shows the baseline Fe-C binary system with characteristic phases including ferrite (α, δ), austenite (γ), and cementite (M₃C), marked with key points S (eutectoid) and E (maximum C solubility in γ). Panel (b) depicts the Fe-15Mn-C system, where Mn addition shifts the high-temperature peritectic reaction toward lower C ranges, significantly extends the γ single-phase field to lower temperatures (reducing the S point to approximately 386°C), and introduces additional carbide phases M₇C₃ and M₂₃C₆ at lower temperatures compared to the binary system. Panel (c) displays the Fe-7Al-C system, demonstrating that Al addition enlarges the (δ+γ) region, shifts the γ single-phase area to higher C concentrations, replaces the (γ+M₃C) field with (γ+κ) and (γ+κ+M₃C) regions featuring κ-carbide precipitation, and includes a dashed line indicating the A2 to B2 disorder-order transition temperature in ferrite. These diagrams collectively demonstrate how Mn and Al, as FCC austenite formers, fundamentally alter phase stability, carbide types, and transformation temperatures, providing critical thermodynamic guidance for designing Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with optimized microstructures.

Figure 6 presents three crystal structure models illustrating the atomic arrangement evolution in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Subfigure (a) depicts the disordered austenite (γ) phase with a face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice, where Fe/Mn and Al atoms are randomly distributed at cubic and face-centered positions. Subfigure (b) shows the ordered γ phase (L1₂ structure), where Al atoms occupy corner positions while Fe and Mn atoms locate at face-centered sites, representing short-range order. Subfigure (c) illustrates the κ-carbide (j-carbide) with E2₁ perovskite crystal structure and ideal stoichiometry (Fe,Mn)₃AlC, where Al occupies each corner, Fe and Mn sit at face centers, and the carbon atom is positioned at the body-centered octahedral interstitial site coordinated by Fe and Mn. The figure demonstrates the structural progression from disordered solid solution to ordered L1₂ and finally to the perovskite κ-carbide through carbon ordering into octahedral interstices, which forms when Al content exceeds 2% and significantly influences precipitation hardening behavior.

The figure presents three isothermal phase sections of Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys at 900°C, illustrating the effect of manganese content on phase stability. Subfigures correspond to (a) Mn = 10 wt%, (b) Mn = 20 wt%, and (c) Mn = 30 wt%, with Al content (0–14 wt%) on the horizontal axis and C content (0–4 wt%) on the vertical axis. The diagrams delineate phase fields including single-phase austenite (γ), γ+M₃C cementite, γ+κ carbide, γ+κ+α ferrite, α+κ, and B2+κ regions. Experimental alloy compositions are marked with symbols indicating their phase fields: open circles (γ), open triangles (γ+κ), open squares (γ+M₃C), solid squares (α+κ), and solid diamonds (κ+α). Experimental boundaries (red lines) are compared against FactSage 6.4 calculations (solid black lines) and CALPHAD approach predictions (dotted lines). The series demonstrates how increasing Mn content expands the γ phase field and shifts carbide stability, validating thermodynamic models for low-density steel design.

Figure 8 presents thermodynamic equilibrium phase diagrams calculated using FactSage 6.4 software, illustrating the temperature-dependent phase evolution in two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel alloys. Subfigure (a) depicts the phase proportions for Fe-13.5Mn-6.3Al-0.78C, while subfigure (b) shows Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C. Both graphs plot phase percentage versus temperature (400-850°C), revealing the competitive stability of austenite (γ), ferrite (α), κ-carbide, and cementite-type carbides (M23C6, M7C3). As temperature increases, γ austenite grows at the expense of α ferrite, with the two-phase region shifting to higher temperatures in the higher-Mn alloy. The κ-carbide phase exhibits distinct stability windows, peaking around 500-600°C before dissolving at elevated temperatures. M23C6 carbides appear at lower temperatures but diminish above 550°C. These calculations demonstrate that increasing Mn and Al content expands the austenite stability field and modifies carbide precipitation kinetics, providing critical guidance for optimizing heat treatment windows in these lightweight steels.

This figure presents three polythermal sections of the Fe-Mn-Al-C quaternary phase diagram calculated using FactSage software, showing the temperature-composition relationships for Fe-15Mn-xAl-yC alloys with fixed 15 wt% Mn and varying Al contents: (a) 4% Al, (b) 7% Al, and (d) 10% Al, as a function of carbon content up to 2.5 wt%. The vertical sections display the stability fields of various phases including liquid (L), austenite (γ), delta-ferrite (δ), B2-ordered phase, κ-carbide, M23C6, M7C3, and cementite (θ). The dash-dotted lines indicate the formation temperatures of the κ phase, while the dashed lines represent the disorder-to-order transition temperatures. As Al content increases from 4% to 10%, the phase diagram topology changes significantly: the γ-loop expands, the B2 phase field becomes more prominent at lower temperatures, and the κ-carbide stability region shifts. These calculations provide essential thermodynamic guidance for designing low-density steel compositions and predicting phase transformations during heat treatment and thermomechanical processing.

This figure presents two calculated polythermal sections of the Fe-Mn-Al-C phase diagram computed using FactSage, illustrating the phase equilibria of low-density steels as functions of aluminum and manganese content. Subfigure (a) displays the phase diagram as a function of Al content (0–12 wt%) with fixed Mn at 20% and C at 0.85%, revealing phase fields including liquid (L), austenite (γ), ferrite (δ, α), and various carbides (M₃C, M₇C₃, M₂₃C₆). Subfigure (b) shows the phase diagram as a function of Mn content (0–30 wt%) with fixed Al at 7% and C at 0.85%, highlighting regions such as γ+α, γ+κ, and B2+κ. The dash-dotted lines indicate the formation temperature of the κ (kappa) carbide phase, while dashed lines represent disorder-to-order transition temperatures (e.g., B2). These diagrams demonstrate the stabilization of austenite at intermediate Mn levels and the precipitation of ordered phases and carbides at lower temperatures, providing critical thermodynamic guidance for alloy design in Fe-Mn-Al-C systems.

This figure presents process variants for producing hot rolled and cold rolled austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel strips, displayed as temperature-time schematic diagrams. The left portion illustrates hot rolling (HR) at 1100-1200°C with a finish rolling temperature (FRT) of 900°C, followed by five distinct processing routes labeled 1 through 5. Route 1 (dashed line) shows direct fast cooling to 500-750°C range followed by slow cooling or isothermal holding, while Route 2 involves fast cooling to room temperature followed by isothermal annealing. The central and right portions depict cold rolling (CR) sequences followed by solution treatment at 900-1100°C in the single austenite phase region, and subsequent annealing at 600-900°C. Routes 3, 4, and 5 represent different aging treatments: Process 3 involves no isothermal holding, while Processes 4 and 5 are designed to maximize age hardening effects through κ-phase precipitation at 500-700°C for 5-20 hours. The diagram illustrates the critical processing windows where solution treatment, recrystallization annealing, and precipitation hardening can be applied to control microstructure and mechanical properties in lightweight austenitic steels.

This figure presents a Time-Temperature-Transformation (TTT) diagram illustrating the decomposition kinetics of γ-solid solution in an Fe-28Mn-8.5Al-1C-1.25Si alloy during isothermal aging. The diagram maps temperature (300–950°C) against logarithmic time (0.01–1000 h), delineating five distinct transformation zones: Area 1 represents the single-phase austenite (γ) region where spinodal decomposition initiates; Area 2 corresponds to homogeneous precipitation of κ′-carbide within the γ matrix (γ+κ′); Area 3 indicates grain boundary precipitation of κ*-carbide (γ+κ′+κ*); Area 4 depicts discontinuous reaction products comprising grain boundary α-ferrite with B2/DO₃ ordering (γ+κ′+κ*+α(B2/DO₃)); and Area 5 shows the equilibrium phase assemblage of γ+κ+B2/DO₃. The C-shaped curves reveal that aging below 500°C significantly extends the time required for grain boundary κ* precipitation, while intermediate temperatures (600–800°C) accelerate carbide formation. The diagram demonstrates that Si addition promotes B2/DO₃ precipitate formation while suppressing β-Mn phase, providing critical kinetic boundaries for controlling microstructural evolution during heat treatment of austenitic low-density steels.

This figure presents three secondary electron SEM micrographs (SEI, 15.0 kV) capturing the progressive microstructural evolution of a Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C austenitic steel during isothermal holding at 700°C for various durations. Subfigure (a) at 1 h shows fine intragranular κ'-carbide precipitation within the austenite matrix and initial cellular transformation products at grain boundaries. Subfigure (b) at 25 h illustrates the development of lamellar (κ+γ+α) colonies growing from austenite grain boundaries via discontinuous precipitation, with distinct phase labeling identifying ferrite (α), κ-carbide, and retained austenite (γ) in alternating plate morphologies. Subfigure (c) at 100 h reveals extensively coarsened lamellar colonies with thick, well-developed α and κ plates penetrating deeply into the matrix. The temporal sequence demonstrates the cellular transformation mechanism where high-temperature austenite decomposes into lamellar constituents, confirming that prolonged aging promotes progressive coarsening of (α+κ+γ) colonies and microstructural degradation in these low-density steels.

Figure 15 presents the microstructural evolution of a Fe-13.5Mn-6.3Al-0.78C low-density steel subjected to solution treatment at 1000 °C followed by aging at 600 °C for 100 h. Panel (a) is an optical micrograph (scale bar 100 μm) revealing large j-pearlite colonies distributed along prior austenite grain boundaries, indicating cellular precipitation at austenite boundaries. Panels (b) and (c) are bright-field TEM images (scale bar 0.2 μm) displaying the internal lamellar structure of various j-pearlites, where labels identify α (ferrite), κ (kappa-carbide), and C representing M23C6 carbide. The images collectively demonstrate the eutectoid decomposition of austenite into a three-phase lamellar mixture (α + κ + M23C6) during isothermal aging. The micrographs confirm that due to the hypereutectoid Al content, proeutectoid ferrite and kappa-carbide form prior to the eutectoid reaction, and the remaining austenite decomposes into the observed lamellar structure, providing direct evidence for complex carbide precipitation and phase transformation mechanisms in aged Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

This figure presents a compositional phase diagram mapping the precipitation behavior of intragranular κ-carbide (kappa-carbide) in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels as a function of alloy composition. The vertical axis indicates carbon content ranging from 0 to 1.6 wt%, while the horizontal axis shows aluminum content from 0 to 12 wt%. Experimental alloy compositions are marked by distinct symbols: open circles represent single-phase austenite (γ), solid squares indicate the two-phase austenite-plus-κ-carbide field (γ+κ), and solid diamonds denote the three-phase region containing austenite, κ-carbide, and α-ferrite (γ+κ+α). Solid lines depict the κ-formation boundaries predicted by Eq. (3) from Huang et al. for 15Mn, 20Mn, and 30Mn steel grades, illustrating that increasing manganese content shifts the precipitation boundary toward higher aluminum and carbon concentrations. Dashed lines indicate the α-ferrite formation boundaries for 15Mn and 30Mn compositions. The diagram reveals that κ-carbide precipitation is promoted by elevated aluminum and carbon levels, while higher manganese stabilizes the austenite phase and delays carbide formation, providing critical guidance for alloy design in lightweight automotive steels.

The figure presents two vertical section phase diagrams illustrating the solvus temperatures of κ-carbides as functions of (a) Al content in Fe-30Mn-xAl-1C alloys and (b) Mn content in Fe-xMn-7Al-1C alloys. The diagrams delineate three phase regions: single-phase austenite (γ), two-phase γ+κ* (intergranular κ-carbide), and three-phase γ+κ*+κ' (intergranular κ* and intragranular κ'-carbide). In panel (a), increasing Al from 5.5 to 10 wt% raises the κ* solvus from approximately 740°C to 850°C and the κ' solvus from ~550°C to 750°C, indicating strong stabilization of carbides by aluminum. In panel (b), increasing Mn from 23.5 to 29.5 wt% slightly decreases both solvus temperatures, with the κ* boundary declining from ~815°C to ~780°C and the κ' boundary from ~690°C to ~665°C. The data points distinguish intergranular κ* (crosses), intragranular κ' (open circles), and the three-phase region (filled circles). These results demonstrate that Al addition significantly elevates the dissolution temperature of both carbide types, while Mn has a comparatively minor inverse effect, providing critical thermodynamic guidance for designing austenitic low-density steels with controlled precipitation hardening.

This figure presents the microstructural characterization and formation mechanism of B2 precipitates in a Fe–15Mn–10Al-0.86C–5Ni low-density steel subjected to 70% cold rolling reduction followed by annealing at 900 °C for 2 minutes. Panel (a) is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) backscattered electron image displaying a multiphase microstructure consisting of austenite matrix and B2 precipitates. Three distinct regions are highlighted: area (1) shows B2 stringer bands transformed from δ-ferrite produced from liquid; area (2) exhibits B2 particles (200–1000 nm) transformed from α-precipitates at γ grain boundaries and edges in recrystallized regions; area (3) shows finer B2 particles (50–300 nm) transformed from α-precipitates at shear bands within γ grains that did not recrystallize. Panel (b) schematically illustrates the formation mechanism, contrasting recrystallized grains (1–2 μm) where B2 precipitates at grain boundaries with larger sizes, versus deformed grains retaining shear bands where B2 precipitation occurs at shear bands with smaller sizes. The figure demonstrates that α-ferrite precipitation during annealing transforms to B2 phase, and the precipitation location and size depend on the recrystallization status, offering a strategy for nanoscale precipitation strengthening.

Figure 19 comprises two micrographs illustrating phase formation during hot rolling in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Panel (a) is an optical micrograph of a Fe-5Mn-6Al-0.4C steel showing α-ferrite formed within the γ phase band (grey areas) during the hot rolling stage, with additional phases (α+κ) formed at coiling temperatures; the inset reveals banded δ and γ phase regions at 25 μm scale. Panel (b) is an SEM micrograph of a Fe-2Mn-8Al-0.2C steel revealing κ-carbide (labeled j phase in caption) precipitated along δ/α boundaries during hot rolling, forming a distinct shell structure surrounding the austenite (γ) phase with fine lamellar morphology. The images demonstrate that during hot working, elemental partitioning of C, Mn, and Al occurs between δ and γ phases, accompanied by γ→α transformation; when C and Mn contents are relatively low (2–6% Mn, 6–8% Al, 0.1–0.3% C), the austenite decomposition temperature is high enough for κ-carbide formation even at hot working temperatures, creating interfacial shell structures. These observations confirm composition-dependent phase evolution mechanisms during thermomechanical processing.

Figure 20 presents microstructural characterization of an air-cooled hot-rolled Fe–9Mn–6Al–0.15C alloy. Panel (a) is an optical micrograph revealing a banded microstructure consisting of partially recrystallized δ-ferrite (white regions), γ-austenite (brown areas), and lath-like phases (gray regions) elongated along the rolling direction, with a scale bar of 100 μm. Panel (b) is a scanning electron micrograph providing higher magnification details of the lath-like phases, identifying the constituent phases as γ-austenite, lath-like α′-martensite, and small globular α-ferrite particles, with a scale bar of 10 μm. The images demonstrate that even under air cooling, the decomposition of austenite to ferrite and κ-carbide is suppressed due to the high Mn content (above 6%), resulting in a metastable microstructure containing martensite and retained austenite rather than equilibrium phases. No κ-carbide precipitates are observed in these air-cooled conditions. The microstructural constituents vary with composition, where the amount of α′-martensite decreases with increasing Mn or C content, highlighting the phase stability control in low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

Figure 21 presents the tensile properties and work hardening behavior of Fe-Al steels as a function of aluminum content (0–25 wt%), illustrating the influence of crystal structure ordering on mechanical performance. Panel (a) displays yield strength (YS), ultimate tensile strength (UTS), uniform elongation (UE), and total elongation (TE) versus Al content across different phase regions (A2, K1, A2+DO3, DO3, B2). The YS increases with Al up to ~13 wt% (~575 MPa) then drops sharply at 13–15% before stabilizing, while UTS rises continuously. Both UE and TE decrease with increasing Al, with a ductile-to-brittle transition occurring at 8–10% Al. Panel (b) shows the work hardening rate measured between 1–1.5% plastic strain, which remains low (<2 GPa) for disordered alloys (<7% Al), peaks sharply at ~16% Al (16 GPa) corresponding to ordered DO3 phase formation, and decreases moderately above 20% Al. These curves demonstrate that solid solution strengthening by Al provides ~40 MPa/wt% increase in ferrite, but ordering phenomena dominate the strain hardening response through superdislocation shearing of ordered phases.

Figure 22 presents engineering stress-strain curves of austenitic and austenite-based duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. Panel (a) illustrates the effect of Al and C contents, displaying curves for 20Mn-10Al-1.5C, 20Mn-11Al-1.0C, 20Mn-10Al-1.0C, and 22Mn-(0,3,6)Al-0.6C alloys solution-treated at 1000°C/1h and water quenched. The Al-free 22Mn-0Al-0.6C steel exhibits the highest strain hardening rate, while increasing Al content (3% to 6%) progressively reduces work hardening; high-Al high-C compositions (e.g., 20Mn-11Al-1.0C) show near-zero or negative strain hardening after yielding yet maintain considerable ductility. Panel (b) demonstrates the influence of κ-carbide and B2 intermetallic phases, comparing 18Mn-10Al-0.9C-5Ni treated at 900°C (showing ultrahigh yield strength >1 GPa), and 28Mn-12Al-1.0C in WQ and aged conditions (550°C/5-46 min), alongside 28Mn-10Al-1.0C in WQ condition exhibiting exceptional total elongation (~100%). The figure supports that Al addition suppresses mechanical twinning and promotes planar glide, while κ-carbide precipitation increases yield strength but degrades strain hardenability, with high-Al steels achieving unique ductility through microband-induced plasticity (MBIP) or shear band mechanisms.

The figure presents true stress and strain hardening rate versus true strain curves obtained from room temperature tensile tests for various Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, illustrating distinct deformation mechanisms. Six curves are plotted for specific compositions: Fe-22Mn-0.6C (dashed line, labeled TWIP) showing the characteristic "hump" associated with deformation twinning and strong microstructure refinement; Fe-30Mn-2Al-1.2C (dotted line) exhibiting multi-stage strain hardening linked to dislocation substructure refinement at lower strains and twinning at higher strains; Fe-30Mn-8Al-1.2C (solid line, labeled DSBR), Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C (red line, labeled MBIP), and Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.9C (blue line, labeled SIP) demonstrating qualitatively similar strain dependence to TWIP steels despite different microstructural mechanisms involving planar glide affected by SROs or κ′-carbides; and Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.7C (black dashed line) showing anomalous monotonic decay of strain hardening rate with increasing strain. The x-axis spans true strain from 0 to 0.8, while the y-axis spans true stress/strain hardening rate from 0 to 3000 MPa. The figure establishes that high-Al content (>7%) austenitic steels suppress mechanical twinning and activate newly discovered plasticity mechanisms (MBIP, DSBR, SIP), though these cannot explain negative strain hardening rates observed in highly alloyed or peak-aged conditions.

This figure presents three-dimensional thermodynamic calculations of stacking fault energy (SFE) maps for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels at room temperature. Subfigure (a) illustrates SFE as a function of carbon (0–1.2 wt%) and manganese (0–50 wt%) contents at different aluminum levels (3, 5, 8, and 11 wt%), showing that increasing Al elevates SFE. Subfigure (b) displays SFE versus aluminum (1–12 wt%) and manganese contents at various carbon levels (0.1–1.5 wt%), demonstrating that carbon strongly increases SFE. Subfigure (c) maps the predicted deformation mechanisms—TRIP, TWIP, and MBIP—within the C-Mn-Al compositional space, delineated by iso-SFE surfaces (20 and 80 mJ/m²) and the kappa-carbide forming boundary. The figure reveals that Al and C additions linearly increase SFE by approximately 10 mJ/m² per wt% Al and 40 mJ/m² per wt% C, respectively, thereby suppressing mechanical twinning and shifting the dominant deformation mechanism from TRIP to TWIP and subsequently to MBIP with increasing Al content.

This scatter plot illustrates the relationship between total elongation (TE, %) and ultimate tensile strength (UTS, MPa) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, tested using uniform specimen geometry conforming to ASTM E-8 standard (50 mm × 12.5 mm × 1 mm). The tensile data represent solution-treated and water-quenched conditions compiled from multiple studies. The graph categorizes alloys into distinct phase-based groups: δ single phase (ferritic), δ-based duplex, γ single phase (labeled as Mn-TWIP), γ-based duplex, and γ+(δ)+coarse κ (including martensitic MS), alongside conventional automotive steels (IF, Mild, IF-HS, BH, CMn, TRIP, DP/CP) for comparison. Data points reveal that γ single-phase austenitic steels achieve superior strength-ductility combinations (UTS ~800-1400 MPa with TE ~30-100%), while δ-based duplex structures exhibit moderate properties (UTS ~400-900 MPa, TE ~20-70%). Alloys containing coarse κ-carbides or martensite (MS) show high strength but limited ductility (TE <10%). Diagonal lines indicate constant UTS×TE products (10-70 GPa%), demonstrating that Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, particularly austenitic TWIP steels, outperform conventional high-strength steels in strength-ductility balance.

This figure presents engineering stress–strain curves comparing the tensile behavior of two ferritic Fe-Al steels (Fe-6.8Al and Fe-8.1Al) and two ferrite-based duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (Fe-3.3Mn-6.6Al-0.2C and Fe-8.5Mn-5.6Al-0.3C) under various heat treatment conditions. The ferritic steels (red curves), annealed at 850°C for 5 min, exhibit lower ultimate tensile strengths (UTS) of approximately 450–500 MPa with total elongations (TE) of 15–30%. In contrast, the ferrite-based duplex steels demonstrate significantly enhanced mechanical properties through phase transformation mechanisms. The Fe-3.3Mn-6.6Al-0.2C steel (blue curves) shows moderate strength (~600 MPa) with improved ductility when subjected to austempering at 400°C for 10 min after annealing at 800°C for 5 min, compared to direct quenching. The Fe-8.5Mn-5.6Al-0.3C steel (black curves) achieves the highest performance, with UTS ranging from 800 to 900 MPa and elongation up to 70%, depending on annealing parameters (800°C/1 min, 800°C/30 min, or 900°C/30 min). The caption and text indicate that the superior strength-ductility balance in the duplex steels arises from TRIP and/or TWIP effects activated during deformation, with specific mechanisms depending on austenite stability, stacking fault energy, and grain characteristics. This figure demonstrates that ferrite-based duplex low-density steels can achieve UTS of 600–950 MPa combined with TE of 30–70%, outperforming simple ferritic Fe-Al alloys and rivaling conventional bake-hardenable and HSLA steels while offering reduced density.

This figure presents the tensile properties of two series of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels as a function of aluminum content, comparing solution-treated and aged conditions. Panel (a) shows Fe-30Mn-1.2C-xAl (high-carbon austenitic/TWIP steel), while panel (b) displays Fe-30Mn-0.4C-xAl (low-carbon duplex steel). All steels were solutionized at 1100°C for 2 hours, with solid curves representing water-quenched samples and dotted curves representing samples subsequently aged at 550°C for 24 hours. Each subplot plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, magenta squares), yield strength (YS, blue diamonds), total elongation (TE, black triangles), and δ (green/yellow inverted triangles) against Al content (0–12 wt%). For the 1.2C series, UTS initially decreases then increases sharply above 8% Al, with significant age-hardening effects only appearing when Al exceeds 8% due to κ-carbide precipitation. The 0.4C series shows minimum strength at 2% Al (fully austenitic, maximum ductility), followed by increasing strength but decreasing elongation as δ-ferrite forms at higher Al contents. The figure demonstrates that Al addition promotes solid-solution strengthening in ferrite and enables precipitation hardening in high-Al austenitic matrices, while excessive ferrite formation suppresses twinning and degrades ductility.

The figure presents tensile property curves as a function of carbon content (0–2 wt%) for two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel series: (a) Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC and (b) Fe-20Mn-10Al-xC. Each subplot displays ultimate tensile strength (UTS, magenta squares), yield strength (YS, blue diamonds), total elongation (TE, black triangles), and δ-ferrite fraction (red crosses, labeled δ), with subplot (b) additionally indicating κ-carbide presence. As carbon increases, UTS rises monotonically from approximately 780 MPa to 1100 MPa in (a) and 760 MPa to 1250 MPa in (b), while YS initially decreases slightly in the duplex region then increases sharply upon transitioning to single-phase austenite. TE exhibits a peak (approximately 85% at ~0.8 wt% C for (a); 60% at ~1.0 wt% C for (b)) corresponding to the transition from duplex (δ+γ) to fully austenitic microstructure, subsequently declining with further carbon addition due to increased κ-carbide precipitation. The δ-phase content decreases with increasing carbon, reaching zero at ~0.7 wt% C for Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC and ~1.0 wt% C for Fe-20Mn-10Al-xC, marking the microstructural transition points. These trends demonstrate that carbon content controls phase stability and precipitation hardening, enabling optimization of strength-ductility balance through composition design.

This figure presents two mechanical property charts showing the compositional dependence of tensile and impact properties in austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Subfigure (a) illustrates the effect of aluminum content (3–10 wt%) on Fe-30Mn-xAl-(0.85,0.95)C steels water-quenched from 1150°C and aged at 550°C for 16 h, plotting ultimate tensile strength (UTS), yield strength (YS), Charpy U-notch impact energy (E_CUN), total elongation (TE), and reduction of area (δ) against Al concentration. Subfigure (b) displays the effect of carbon content (0.3–1.2 wt%) on Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC steels processed under similar conditions (water-quenched from 1050°C with dashed curves representing as-quenched state and solid curves representing aged condition at 550°C for 16 h). In (a), both UTS and YS remain relatively constant below 7 wt% Al (~800 MPa and ~600 MPa respectively for 0.95% C alloy) but increase sharply above 7 wt% Al, reaching ~1250 MPa UTS at 9–10 wt% Al, while impact energy and ductility peak at intermediate Al levels (~6–7 wt%) then decline. In (b), UTS and YS increase monotonically with carbon content, whereas impact energy and elongation maximize at approximately 0.7 wt% C (~130 J/cm² E_CUN and ~60% TE) before decreasing at higher carbon levels. These trends demonstrate that pronounced strengthening through fine dispersed κ′-carbide precipitation occurs only when Al exceeds 7 wt% and C exceeds 0.7 wt%, but excessive carbide volume fractions degrade ductility and toughness, establishing optimal compositional windows of Al 7–10 wt% and C ~0.7 wt% for balanced mechanical properties in age-hardenable austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys.

This chart compares the precipitation strengthening potential of κ-carbides in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels against conventional microalloying carbides (NbC, VC, TiC) used in microalloyed steels. The x-axis shows precipitation fraction (volume fraction) on a logarithmic scale ranging from 0.0001 to 1, while the y-axis indicates precipitation strengthening in MPa from 0 to 500. The data reveals that κ-carbides provide strengthening between approximately 100 and 350 MPa at relatively high precipitation fractions of 0.05–0.35. In contrast, TiC exhibits the highest strengthening potential (up to ~450 MPa) but at much lower precipitation fractions (~0.0015–0.003), while NbC and VC show intermediate strengthening effects (~70–180 MPa and ~0–120 MPa, respectively) at low volume fractions (~0.0003–0.001). The figure illustrates that although κ-carbides have lower strengthening efficiency per unit volume fraction compared to TiC, they contribute significant age-hardening effects through homogeneous precipitation within austenite at higher volume fractions, which is critical for the strength-ductility balance in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

This figure presents aging curves of austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels established through hardness measurements and tensile tests, illustrating the precipitation hardening kinetics and mechanical property evolution during isothermal aging. Subfigure (a) displays Brinell hardness versus aging time (0.1–1000 h) for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-0.35Si steel at 450°C, 500°C, 550°C, and 600°C, demonstrating that higher aging temperatures accelerate the hardening kinetics but reduce the peak hardness due to earlier overaging. Subfigure (b) compares aging curves of six different alloys aged at 550°C—including Fe-30Mn-7.8Al-1.3C, Fe-34Mn-11Al-0.98C, Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C, Fe-31Mn-7.8Al-0.88C, Fe-29Mn-4.9Al-0.98C, and Fe-34Mn-7.1Al-0.5C—showing that increased Al and C contents enhance the age hardening effect and increase peak hardness. Subfigure (c) plots tensile properties versus aging time for Fe-30Mn-8.5Al-2.0C (550°C), Fe-28Mn-9Al-1.8C (450°C), and Fe-30Mn-7.8Al-1.3C (550°C), where UTS and YS reach maxima at intermediate aging times while total elongation (TE) progressively decreases. The figure establishes that κ'-carbide precipitation provides the strengthening mechanism, with peak properties requiring substantial aging times (10–100 h), and highlights the trade-off between strength and ductility controlled by alloy chemistry and thermal history.

Figure 32 presents engineering stress–strain curves for two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels under various cold rolling and annealing conditions, illustrating the optimization of mechanical properties through thermomechanical processing. Subfigure (a) displays curves for a Fe-22.5Mn-1.2Al-0.3C steel (non-age-hardenable austenitic grade) subjected to 30% or 50% cold rolling (CR) followed by annealing at 550–700°C for various durations (10–30 min), where the CR50%+550°C/30m condition achieves the highest strength (~1100 MPa) but limited ductility. Subfigure (b) shows curves for a Fe-22Mn-7Al-0.8C steel after 50% cold rolling and recrystallization annealing at 800–975°C for 1–25 min, demonstrating that lower annealing temperatures (800–850°C) and shorter times yield higher strength with continuous yielding, while increasing temperature and duration promotes recrystallization, shifting to discontinuous yielding and enhancing elongation up to ~60%. The caption states that these curves support the strategy of combining cold rolling with annealing to obtain high strength and formability simultaneously through grain refinement and controlled recovery of deformation twins.

This figure presents Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy as a function of test temperature for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, comparing solution-treated and aged conditions against conventional stainless steel benchmarks. Curve 1 shows Fe-28Mn-5Al-1C solutionized at 1050°C for 75 min (grain size d = 41 μm), while Curve 2 shows the same composition solutionized for 8 h (d = 135 μm); both display high impact energies (~200 J) at room temperature comparable to austenitic stainless steels but exhibit ductile-to-brittle transition below -100°C. Curve 3 represents Fe-30Mn-10Al-1C-1Si solutionized at 1050°C for 1 h (d = 120 μm), showing similar high-temperature toughness. Curves 4 and 5 demonstrate the detrimental effect of aging at 550°C for 16 h: Curve 4 (Fe-28Mn-5Al-1C) shows significantly reduced impact energy that decreases continuously with temperature, while Curve 5 (Fe-30Mn-10Al-1C-1Si) exhibits near-zero toughness. The shaded background regions indicate typical impact energy ranges for austenitic, duplex, ferritic, and martensitic stainless steels. The figure illustrates that κ-carbide precipitation during aging causes severe embrittlement, reducing impact toughness from excellent levels matching austenitic stainless steels to values comparable to martensitic grades.

Scatter plot comparing specific energy absorption values of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels against conventional deep-drawing steels as a function of aluminum content. The y-axis displays specific energy absorption in J/mm³ ranging from 0.0 to 0.6, while the x-axis shows Al content in weight percent from 6 to 12. Conventional deep-drawing steels—including IF steel, HC300LA, and DC04, represented by distinct square symbols at approximately 6 wt% Al—exhibit specific energy absorption values between 0.16 and 0.25 J/mm³. In contrast, Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, depicted as green diamonds tested at deformation rates of 10³/s and 10⁴/s, demonstrate significantly higher energy absorption capacity, clustering around 0.5 J/mm³ when Al content exceeds 7 wt%, with individual data points ranging from approximately 0.43 to 0.55 J/mm³. The figure illustrates that Fe-Mn-Al-C steels achieve approximately 50% higher specific energy absorption than conventional deep-drawing steels at crash-relevant strain rates, a performance level comparable to Mn-TWIP steels. This enhancement is attributed to severe adiabatic shear band formation during high-strain-rate deformation, as evidenced by the elevated energy absorption values at Al contents above 7 wt%, highlighting the suitability of these alloys for automotive body and frame structures requiring superior crashworthiness.

This figure presents low-cycle fatigue cyclic response curves showing stress amplitude versus number of cycles for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The graph compares two distinct alloy systems: Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C (dotted curves, reference [161]) and Fe-30Mn-10Al-0.4C (solid curves, reference [162]). For the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C austenitic alloy, three aging conditions are shown—550°C/24 h, 710°C/2 h, and 550°C/2 h—all tested at a fixed total strain amplitude of 0.8%. These curves exhibit pronounced cyclic softening behavior, with the 550°C/24 h aged specimen showing the highest initial stress amplitude (approximately 1050 MPa decaying to 750 MPa), attributed to planar slip, shearing of j-carbides, and precipitate dissolution. For the duplex Fe-30Mn-10Al-0.4C alloy (65% austenite + 35% ferrite), six total strain amplitudes are presented ranging from 0.2% to 2.0%. At low strain amplitudes (0.2–0.4%), the material shows cyclic softening; at intermediate amplitudes (0.8–1.0%), it exhibits initial cyclic hardening followed by softening; while at high amplitudes (1.5–2.0%), rapid hardening occurs before failure. The figure demonstrates that aging treatments significantly influence the cyclic stability of austenitic Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C, while the duplex Fe-30Mn-10Al-0.4C displays strain-amplitude-dependent cyclic deformation mechanisms, with fatigue crack initiation occurring in the ferrite phase.
